z=RegressionTests.CheckWorkaroundForBuggySqliteTransferBindingsc
        pysqlite used to segfault with SQLite versions 3.5.x. These return NULL
        for "no-operation" statements

z#RegressionTests.CheckEmptyStatementc
        pysqlite until 2.4.1 did not rebuild the row_cast_map when recompiling
        a statement. This test exhibits the problem.
        z
select * from foor

insert into foo(bar) values (?)r4
create table foo(bar integer)
insert into foo(bar) values (5)N)

z3RegressionTests.CheckConnectionConstructorCallCheckc
        Verifies that subclassed cursor classes are correctly registered with
        the connection object, too.  (fetch-across-rollback problem)

InterfaceErrorrf
CheckCursorRegistration
z'RegressionTests.CheckCursorRegistrationc
        Verifies that creating a connection in autocommit mode works.
        2.5.3 introduced a regression so that these could no longer
        be created.
        r
CheckAutoCommit
RegressionTests.CheckAutoCommitc
        Verifies that running a PRAGMA statement that does an autocommit does
        work. This did not work in 2.5.3/2.5.4.
        r3
pragma page_sizeN)
rowr

z%RegressionTests.CheckPragmaAutocommitc
        Call a connection with a non-string SQL request: check error handling
        of the statement constructor.
        rX
CheckConnectionCall
z#RegressionTests.CheckConnectionCallc
collation_cb
z4RegressionTests.CheckCollation.<locals>.collation_cbu
create_collation)
CheckCollation
RegressionTests.CheckCollationc
        http://bugs.python.org/issue10811
        Recursively using a cursor, such as when reusing it from a generator led to segfaults.
        Now we catch recursive cursor usage and raise a ProgrammingError.
        r
create table a (bar)z
create table b (baz)c
insert into a (bar) values (?))
foo,
z4RegressionTests.CheckRecursiveCursorUse.<locals>.fooz
insert into b (baz) values (?)c
	<genexpr>2
z:RegressionTests.CheckRecursiveCursorUse.<locals>.<genexpr>N)
CheckRecursiveCursorUse
z'RegressionTests.CheckRecursiveCursorUsec
        http://bugs.python.org/issue14720
        The microsecond parsing of convert_timestamp() should pad with zeros,
        since the microsecond string "456" actually represents "456000".
        r
CREATE TABLE t (x TIMESTAMP)z4INSERT INTO t (x) VALUES ('2012-04-04 15:06:00.456')z:INSERT INTO t (x) VALUES ('2012-04-04 15:06:00.123456789')z
SELECT * FROM tc
zKRegressionTests.CheckConvertTimestampMicrosecondPadding.<locals>.<listcomp>i
valuesr
'CheckConvertTimestampMicrosecondPadding4
z7RegressionTests.CheckConvertTimestampMicrosecondPaddingc
CheckInvalidIsolationLevelTypeN
z.RegressionTests.CheckInvalidIsolationLevelTypec
select 1z	select 1
select 2z	select 2
CheckNullCharacterT
z"RegressionTests.CheckNullCharacterc
        Connection.commit() did reset cursors, which made sqlite3
        to return rows multiple times when fetched from cursors
        after commit. See issues 10513 and 23129 for details.
        r
        create table t(c);
        create table t2(c);
        insert into t values(0);
        insert into t values(1);
        insert into t values(2);
        r7
select c from t)
insert into t2(c) values (?)rX
z'should have returned exactly three rows)	r
executescriptr'
	enumerater
counterr%

z&RegressionTests.CheckCommitCursorResetc
        The interpreter shouldn't crash in case Cursor.__init__() is called
        more than once.
        c
